medical_questions.what_is_the_longest_time_it_took_for_ful: About 10 days. I was held out of practice and games for a week and a half after a mild concussion during a JV soccer match in September 2023. I had headaches and some trouble concentrating for the first 4–5 days, then symptoms gradually resolved. I followed the school’s return-to-play protocol with our athletic trainer and my pediatrician, increasing activity step by step until I had no symptoms with full exertion.

explanations_and_signatures.explain_all_yes_answers_here:
Persona: Student-athlete is a 16-year-old female, junior at Topeka High School in Kansas, named Emily Rose Carter. She plays varsity soccer (midfielder) and JV basketball (guard). No major chronic medical conditions. Generally healthy and active, with one prior mild concussion and one episode of heat illness.

Concussion or head injury (Yes):
In early September 2023, during a JV soccer game for Topeka High School, I went up for a header at the same time as another player. We collided heads and I fell backward, hitting the turf. I did not lose consciousness, but I felt immediately dazed and a little confused about the score and what part of the game we were in. I also developed a pretty strong headache within about 10–15 minutes and felt more sensitive to the bright stadium lights.

Our athletic trainer pulled me from the game right away and did a sideline concussion check. Because I was still having a headache and felt "foggy," my parents took me to my pediatrician the next morning. My doctor diagnosed a mild concussion, told me to rest from screens and intense schoolwork for a few days, and not to return to sports until I was completely symptom-free and had gone through the school’s return-to-play protocol.

For the first 3–4 days after the injury, I had headaches that were worse with reading and looking at my phone for too long. I also felt more tired than usual and needed to nap after school. I did not have vomiting, seizures, or any weakness or numbness. My concentration was a little off in class for that first week, so my teachers gave me extra time on a couple of assignments.

By about day 7 my headaches had mostly resolved, and I could do full schoolwork without symptoms. Our athletic trainer then started me on a stepwise return-to-play protocol: light stationary biking, then jogging, then non-contact drills, and finally full practice. I did each step for at least 24 hours and only moved forward if I had no symptoms. I did not have any return of headaches or dizziness during this progression.

Overall, it took about 10 days from the date of the injury until I was fully cleared to return to game play. I was last officially released back to full sports participation by my pediatrician and athletic trainer around 2023-09-28. Since then, I have not had any lingering issues—no chronic headaches, concentration problems, or memory problems. I have played multiple full soccer and basketball seasons since then without any repeat head injuries.

Heat illness (Yes):
In late August 2024, during pre-season varsity soccer conditioning, we had an afternoon practice on a particularly hot and humid day (the heat index was in the upper 90s). We were doing repeated sprint drills, and I started to feel unusually lightheaded and queasy. I also noticed I was getting chills even though it was very hot, and my heart was pounding harder than usual. I realized I hadn’t been drinking enough water earlier in the day.

I told my coach and sat out. Our athletic trainer checked me, had me lie down in the shade, and gave me cool water and an electrolyte drink. She also put cool towels on the back of my neck and had me remove my practice pinnie so I could cool off faster. I did not pass out or vomit, but I felt pretty shaky and nauseated for about 20–30 minutes. After resting and rehydrating, I gradually felt better.

The trainer told me it was likely mild heat exhaustion and emphasized the importance of hydrating throughout the day (not just at practice), eating enough before practice, and taking more frequent water breaks on very hot days. Since then, I’ve been much more careful about drinking water (I carry a 32 oz water bottle to school and refill it twice) and making sure I have a snack with some carbs and salt before practice. I have not had any repeat episodes of heat illness since that day.

Vision problems (Yes):
I have mild nearsightedness (myopia) and have worn glasses since 7th grade, mainly for seeing the board at school and for driving. My most recent eye exam was in June 2024 with Dr. Melissa Nguyen at Topeka Family Eye Care. My prescription is around -1.75 in both eyes. I do not have any history of eye disease, eye surgery, or serious eye injuries.

For sports, I usually wear soft contact lenses instead of glasses because they’re more comfortable and safer on the field. I do not currently wear sports goggles or a face shield, but my contacts give me clear vision for soccer and basketball. I have no problems with double vision, sudden vision loss, or anything like that.

Worry about weight (Yes) and trying to gain or lose weight / special diet (Yes):
As a competitive athlete, I think about my weight and body composition quite a bit, mostly in terms of performance. I’m about 5'6" and currently around 135 lbs. My coaches have talked to our team about fueling properly for sports, and I’ve read a lot online about nutrition for female athletes. Sometimes I worry about whether I’m in the “right” range for my position (midfielder in soccer and guard in basketball), especially since there can be pressure to be lean but also strong and powerful.

I have never been diagnosed with an eating disorder, and I have never engaged in extreme dieting, purging, or abusing laxatives or diet pills. I do, however, pay attention to what I eat and have tried to make small changes to improve my performance and energy levels. For example, over the last year I’ve:
- Focused on eating more protein (eggs, chicken, Greek yogurt, beans) to support muscle recovery.
- Tried to cut back on soda and super sugary snacks on school days because they make me feel sluggish at practice.
- Made an effort to eat breakfast every morning (usually oatmeal with fruit or peanut butter toast and a banana) because I used to skip it and then feel lightheaded by late morning.

I also follow a partial special diet in that I avoid most dairy because it tends to upset my stomach, especially before running. I’m not medically diagnosed as lactose intolerant, but milk, ice cream, and some cheeses make me bloated and sometimes give me mild cramps. Because of that, I usually choose lactose-free milk or almond milk and limit heavy dairy foods, especially before practices or games.

Additionally, my pediatrician suggested I aim to maintain my weight in a healthy range and focus on strength rather than trying to be “as light as possible.” She emphasized that my menstrual cycles are regular, which is a good sign that I’m eating enough for my activity level. I check in with my mom about my eating habits, and she helps plan meals so I’m getting a balance of carbs, protein, and healthy fats.

Overall, while I do think and worry about my weight and performance, I feel it’s under control and I’m open with my parents and doctor about it. I do not restrict food in a dangerous way or over-exercise beyond my regular team practices and reasonable conditioning.

Special diet details:
- I avoid most dairy products, especially within 3–4 hours of intense exercise, due to stomach discomfort.
- I try to avoid super greasy fast food on game days because it has made me feel heavy and sluggish in the past.
- I intentionally add snacks like granola bars, fruit, and trail mix between school and practice to avoid low energy.

Gender identity (Female):
I currently identify as female and have always identified as female. My pronouns are she/her. This is consistent with my school records and how my coaches and teammates know me.

Menstrual history (Yes to having periods):
I had my first menstrual period at age 12, near the end of 7th grade. My cycles are fairly regular, usually about every 28–32 days. I do get mild cramps and some lower back discomfort on the first day of my period, but it has not interfered with my ability to participate in sports. I usually take ibuprofen with food if the cramps are bothersome and use a heating pad at home if needed.

In the last 12 months, I’ve had about 11 periods. I missed one month during a particularly intense stretch of soccer and finals week in May where I was really stressed and probably not sleeping enough. I mentioned this to my pediatrician at my last well visit, and she said that a single missed period can happen with stress and increased activity but to let her know if it becomes a pattern or if I start missing multiple cycles. Since then, my periods have returned to their usual timing.

I am not currently experiencing any menstrual problems that affect my athletic participation. I plan ahead by making sure I have supplies (pads/tampons) in my locker and sports bag and by staying hydrated, which seems to help with cramps and bloating.

Heat illness, concussion, and weight/menstrual history are all being monitored:
My parents, coaches, and pediatrician are aware of my prior mild concussion and the one episode of heat illness, and they are comfortable with me continuing in competitive sports. We have talked about:
- Speaking up immediately if I have any new head injury or concussion symptoms.
- Hydrating aggressively before and during practices/games, especially in late summer.
- Fueling adequately and not restricting food in a way that could lead to low energy availability or menstrual changes.

If needed, I am open to meeting with a sports dietitian or counselor in the future to keep things on a healthy track.
